title: Adding woody material to a compost heap
subtitle: How to use branches, wood chips, and prunings without stalling the heap
excerpt: Woody prunings and wood chips have a very high carbon-to-nitrogen ratio and break down slowly in a standard compost bin. Adding them without preparation slows or stalls the heap. This guide covers chipping, layering, and the better uses for woody waste.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic guidance on composting woody material. Royal Horticultural Society guidance on using wood chip as mulch. WRAP Recycle Now composting technical factsheet.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fresh wood chips have a C:N ratio of approximately 300-500:1. Freshly chipped green prunings are lower (around 60-80:1). The ideal compost C:N is 25:1. Woody material locks up nitrogen in the heap while bacteria slowly break down the cellulose.

Woody material is the brownest of the browns: very high carbon, very low nitrogen. Balancing it requires proportionally large quantities of nitrogen-rich greens, which is rarely practical in a home compost bin.

A managed hot heap generating internal temperatures of 55-70°C can process chipped woody material much faster than a cold compost bin, because thermophilic bacteria actively break down cellulose and lignin at high temperatures.
Partially decomposed leaves composted separately. A separate pen for woody material, similar to a leaf mould pen, allows slow decomposition without stalling the main compost bin.

content.text: of 300 to 500 for dried wood and 60 to 80 for fresh green prunings. Compared with the 25:1 target for active composting, even fresh prunings are heavily biased toward carbon. Adding a large volume of prunings to a standard cold compost bin ties up nitrogen and can stall decomposition for months.
What can go in the main compost bin

Soft annual plant stems, spent vegetable plants, and green hedge trimmings up to pencil thickness can go into a cold compost bin in thin layers, alternated with nitrogen-rich material. Break or chop them into 10-15 cm lengths first. Do not add more than one thin layer at a time. Thicker woody stems and branches need a different approach.

Wood chip path or mulch: run branches through a shredder and use the chips directly as a path surface or as a 50-100 mm deep weed-suppressing mulch around shrubs and trees. The chips break down in place over 2-3 years without stalling anything.
Separate woody compost pen: a simple wooden frame or wire cage where chipped woody material is piled and left for 2-3 years. It functions like a
pen but takes longer. The resulting material is good for improving soil structure.
Hot compost heap: if you manage a
heap, chipped woody material at up to 25% of total volume is manageable. The high temperatures accelerate cellulose breakdown. Balance with large quantities of nitrogen-rich material.
When woody material has stalled the bin

If a heap has been filled with branches and has stopped decomposing, the options are: (1) remove the woody material to a separate pen for slow breakdown; (2) add very large quantities of nitrogen-rich material (fresh grass, high-nitrogen activator, nettle or comfrey leaves) to rebalance the
; (3) wait: the fungi will eventually break down woody material regardless, but it takes years rather than months.

attrs.title: Fresh woodchip and nitrogen draw-down
attrs.body: Fresh wood chip laid directly on a vegetable bed as mulch can temporarily draw nitrogen from the topsoil as soil bacteria consume the chips. This effect is limited to the soil-chip interface and rarely affects crops. To avoid any risk, age the chips for six months before using on vegetable beds, or use them only around perennial shrubs and trees.
